one_line_takeaway: HALLUWORLD is a benchmark-construction paper (not a security paper) that uses fully-specified synthetic/semi-synthetic 'reference worlds' (gridworld, chess, terminal) to automatically label hallucinations and shows that while frontier LLMs have largely solved direct perceptual read-out, they still hallucinate on multi-step memory, causal forward-simulation, and epistemic abstention -- and that extended chain-of-thought reasoning does not reliably reduce, and can worsen, these failures.
key_finding: Perceptual hallucination on directly-observed information is near-solved for frontier models (near-ceiling accuracy on presence probes), but multi-step state tracking (Memory) and causal forward simulation (Causal) remain hard even for frontier models, and extended thinking does not reliably fix this -- it can even worsen causal hallucination (e.g., Sonnet 4.6 Causal: 19.7%->24.0% and Opus 4.6 Causal: 20.1%->25.2% with thinking, in HALLUWORLD-GRID). A consistent category hierarchy (U < P < M < C, with X high-variance) held in grid; in terminal, Perceptual~=Causal > Cross-Tier~=Memory >> Uncertainty, with Uncertainty the hardest category everywhere it is tested (best models, e.g. GPT-5.5/o3, cap at 76.9% accuracy on terminal uncertainty probes). Models consistently over-trust written/textual testimony over direct observation (e.g., Opus 4.6 hallucinates more with an accurate notice board present: 42.0% vs. 28.4% without, in level C1a). In chess, the two long-horizon categories (Memory, Compound/X) separate frontier models sharply even when Perceptual/Causal are near-saturated (e.g., GPT-5.4-mini with thinking: P 7.3%, C 13.0% but M 75.0%, X 36.0%). The paper concludes hallucinations are qualitatively distinct failure modes (not one underlying capability) and that controllable reference-world environments give a reproducible way to isolate and study them.
core_contribution: The paper introduces HALLUWORLD, an extensible benchmark framework that operationalizes LLM hallucination as observable error with respect to an explicit 'reference world' formulation (a reference world W = (S,H,R), a view function V controlling what the model can observe, and a truth/conflict-resolution function T_{W,P}). Building on this formal definition (adapted from prior work by Liu et al.), the authors instantiate three fully synthetic/semi-synthetic environments -- HALLUWORLD-GRID (MiniGrid-based gridworlds, 33 levels / 839 probes), HALLUWORLD-CHESS (Lichess FEN positions, 7 levels / 350 probes), and HALLUWORLD-TERMINAL (grounded in 132 TERMINAL-BENCH agent trajectories over 110 software-engineering tasks, 529 probes) -- in which ground-truth hallucination labels are generated automatically from environment/simulator state rather than by human annotation. Probes are organized into five cognitive-skill categories: Perceptual (P), Memory (M), Causal (C), Uncertainty (U), and Compound/Cross-category (X). The paper benchmarks a dozen-plus frontier and open-weight LLMs (thinking and non-thinking variants) across all three domains and reports fine-grained, category-level hallucination rates rather than a single aggregate score.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or security threat model. The one nominally 'adversarial' design element is benign robustness testing: gridworld notice-boards/signposts that can contradict direct observation, and chess FEN strings with two pieces deliberately transposed, both used as controlled manipulations of a 'conflict policy' to study whether models over-trust textual testimony over direct observation -- this is a study of model epistemic robustness/calibration, not a security attack.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) probing measures only explicit/stated model beliefs, not internal representations, which may diverge (suggesting mechanistic/causal-tracing follow-up); (2) probe placement is currently hand/co-designed with each environment rather than discovered via active learning for maximal cognitive load; (3) multi-source conflict resolution is not yet explicitly/fully assessed; (4) no evaluation in small/data-limited model regimes (e.g., BabyLM-scale). Additionally, within the terminal results, the thinking-vs-non-thinking comparison is confounded by an unequal compute budget (thinking models given 16,384 output tokens vs. 256 for standard models), which the authors explicitly flag as making the gap an upper bound rather than a controlled comparison. The terminal environment's complexity/noisiness is also noted by the authors as making it harder to isolate specific failure modes compared to gridworld/chess.

datasets_used: HALLUWORLD-GRID: 33 unique MiniGrid-based levels, 839 probe questions
HALLUWORLD-CHESS: 7 unique levels, 350 probes, positions drawn from curated Lichess FEN strings
HALLUWORLD-TERMINAL: 529 probes derived from 132 TERMINAL-BENCH trajectories spanning 110 software-engineering tasks
quantitative_results: Grid overall hallucination rate range: reasoning/thinking frontier models 5.0-8.1% (e.g. GPT-5.5 (T) 5.0%, Claude Opus 4.6 5.9%, o3 6.8%); non-reasoning frontier/open models cluster 12.6-15.4% (e.g. GPT-4o 12.6%, DeepSeek-V3-0324 15.4%); weakest model GPT-4o-mini 28.1%
Grid Causal category is hardest across frontier models: Claude Opus 4.6 20.1%, GPT-5.5 25.3%, Kimi K2.6 47.4% (anomalous outlier)
Grid thinking paradox: Sonnet 4.6 Perceptual 2.8%->1.1% (improves) but Causal 19.7%->24.0% (worsens) with thinking; Opus 4.6 Perceptual 3.7%->1.7% but Causal 20.1%->25.2%
Grid Hard subset (12 level/serializer pairs): frontier models 15.5-21.3% mean hallucination; Kimi K2.6 outlier at 53.9%, driven by C1b variants at 95.0% and 100.0%
Grid C1a (notice board) probe: Opus 4.6 42.0% hallucination with accurate board present vs. 28.4% without
Grid C6 (Flood-Fire Escape) among hardest levels: Sonnet 4.6 56.1% hallucination
Grid C3 (Flood Room) split within frontier tier: Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 achieve 0%, while o3 reaches 43.3% and o4-mini 33.3%
Chess No-FEN overall hallucination: GPT-5.5 (T) 0.3%, o3 2.0%, GPT-5.4 2.6%, o4-mini 5.7%, o3-mini 18.7%, Sonnet 4.6 (T) 19.8% ... up to GPT-4o-mini 62.9% (weakest)
Chess: adding reasoning budget to gpt-5.4-mini cuts hallucination on Perceptual (49.3%->7.3%) and Causal (52.0%->13.0%) but leaves Memory at 75.0% and Compound/X at 36.0%

Chess frontier separation on long-horizon categories: GPT-5.5 solves Memory and X at 0.0%; o3 near-saturated (M 2.1%, X 6.0%); gpt-5.4 (M 8.3%, X 4.0%); Claude thinking models strong on M/X but high on Causal (Opus 4.6 (T) Causal 48.0%, Sonnet 4.6 (T) Causal 44.0%)
Chess Incorrect-FEN robustness perturbation: top models (gpt-5.5, o3) show a 3-4 percentage-point increase in hallucination versus No-FEN condition

Terminal overall hallucination: GPT-5.5 (T) 5.9% (94.1% overall accuracy, no model saturates any probe category) up to Kimi-K2.6 56.5%; two tiers -- thinking/reasoning models 5.9-21.0%, standard models 26.3-56.5%
Terminal Uncertainty is hardest category throughout: even GPT-5.5 and o3 cap at 76.9% accuracy (i.e. hallucinate ~1/4 of the time when correct answer is 'cannot determine')
Terminal GPT-4o-mini inverted memory profile: Memory hallucination 57.4% substantially exceeds Causal 39.6%
Terminal Kimi-K2.6 highest Uncertainty hallucination in terminal domain: 73.1%

Terminal thinking-depth ablation: Sonnet 4.6 accuracy rises monotonically from 79.2% (no thinking) to 86.2% (max thinking); GPT-5.5 jumps +8.5pp from 86.4% to 94.9% but non-monotonically across reasoning-effort levels, peaking at 'high' (84.3% on Uncertainty specifically) and regressing slightly at 'xhigh'
Terminal probe breakdown by failure mode: stale memory 187 probes, cross-category reasoning 110, uncertainty overclaim 107, causal shortcut 104, version/API hallucination 21
baselines_compared: Non-thinking: GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5.4-mini, GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.6, DeepSeek-V3.1, GLM-5, Qwen-3-30B, Kimi K2.6
Thinking: o3-mini, o4-mini, o3, GPT-5.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6 (T), Claude Opus 4.6 (T), plus GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.4-mini (T) appearing in chess/terminal tables

No external prior benchmark is used as a numeric baseline for comparison -- the paper is itself a new benchmark; prior hallucination benchmarks (HalluLens, MIRAGE-BENCH, AGENTHALLU, RAGBench, etc.) are discussed only in Related Work as motivation/contrast, not as quantitative baselines evaluated in this paper's own experiments.
